packTitle: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks Import Batch
notes: Normalized from Perplexity category output for Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks.
Planning, signaling, and practical kit content is intentionally prioritized higher than advanced or judgment-heavy field skills.
Last-resort untreated-water guidance, knots, and knife-use content are included conservatively and generally marked needs_source_review or lower-ranked.
Knot guidance is explicitly non-technical and must never be interpreted as climbing, rescue, or life-safety rope instruction.

categories.categoryName: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
categories.summary: Core field decision-making, simple rescue signaling, water collection, hygiene, basic cordage, safe tool use, and emergency kit guidance for public-facing survival and stranded-user scenarios.

guides.title: STOP-Think-Observe-Plan (First 30 Minutes)
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: First 30 Minutes: Stay Oriented and Make a Simple Plan
guides.quickAnswer: Pause, calm down, check for injuries and hazards, then make a simple plan instead of walking blindly.
guides.whenToUse: Any time you realize you are lost, stranded, off-route, or unexpectedly stuck outdoors.
guides.preferredAction: Use the STOP method, then act on the safest, simplest short plan.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot think clearly, focus only on immediate safety: move out of danger, treat obvious bleeding, get out of wind or rain, then rest and reassess.
steps: Stop in a safe spot away from immediate hazards such as rockfall, traffic, avalanche terrain, surf, or fire.
Breathe slowly for 30 to 60 seconds to reduce panic.
Check yourself and others quickly for serious bleeding, breathing problems, or inability to move.
Observe the time of day, weather, terrain, nearby landmarks, last known point, and any cell signal or GPS information.
Ask what the main threat is in the next few hours: cold, heat, darkness, dehydration, or injury.
Make a simple 15 to 30 minute plan such as staying put, improving shelter, and signaling for help.
If possible, text or call emergency services and a trusted contact with your situation and last known location.
Carry out the short plan, then pause and reassess.
warnings: Panic and aimless wandering are major causes of worsening lost-person outcomes.
Poor decisions made in the first 30 minutes can be hard to reverse in bad terrain or bad weather.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking just to do something if you are unsure where you are.
Do not ignore injuries or severe cold or heat to push on a bit more.
Do not rely only on your memory of the route if you are clearly disoriented.
redFlags: Severe bleeding, difficulty breathing, altered mental status, or inability to walk.
Rapidly worsening weather with poor shelter or warm or dry clothing.
Nightfall close at hand with no light, no clear route, or no shelter.
Immediate terrain hazards such as flooding, avalanche slopes, cliffs, or surf.
preparednessTips: Share a trip plan with a trusted person including route and return time.
Carry a whistle, map, compass, charged phone, power bank, and simple emergency kit.
Practice the STOP method on day hikes so it feels automatic under stress.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear public guidance on what to do if you get lost, including stopping, assessing, and staying put.

sourceReferences.title: Lost Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical guidance on staying found and staying put once lost.

sourceReferences.title: What should you do if you are lost during a hike
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Accessible STOP-based guidance for lost hikers.

guides.title: Decide: Stay Put or Move Carefully
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: First 30 Minutes: Stay Oriented and Make a Simple Plan
guides.quickAnswer: If you are truly lost but not in immediate danger, staying put is usually safer and makes you easier to find; move only to escape danger or reach clearly safer ground.
guides.whenToUse: When you realize you are off-route or stranded and are deciding whether to stay or try to self-rescue.
guides.preferredAction: Stay put near your last known location, improve shelter, and make yourself visible unless there is an immediate life threat where you are.
guides.backupAction: If staying is clearly unsafe, move carefully to the nearest safer, more visible, and more sheltered area, then stop and re-establish your plan.
steps: Identify immediate dangers such as rising water, unstable slopes, falling rocks, fire, lightning exposure, or traffic.
If no immediate danger exists, stay near your last known location where rescuers will likely search first.
Improve shelter and visibility in a spot that is safer but still findable.
If you must move, choose a conservative destination such as slightly higher ground above flood level or a safer forest edge.
Move slowly and avoid steep drop-offs, fast water crossings, avalanche paths, and dense terrain.
Once in a safer spot, stop again, mark your location if possible, and begin signaling.
warnings: Wandering far from your last known route can drastically enlarge search areas and delay rescue.
Self-rescue over difficult terrain without skills or equipment leads to many injuries and fatalities.
whatNotToDo: Do not descend blindly into gullies, canyons, or rivers just because downhill might lead to people.
Do not cross fast-moving water above your knees without training and proper gear.
Do not split your group unless absolutely necessary.
redFlags: Very poor shelter options with severe weather imminent.
Active fire, unstable ground, or advancing water in your area.
A severely injured person who cannot be moved safely from a dangerous location.
preparednessTips: Learn local terrain hazards before trips.
Carry a paper map and compass and know basic use.
Tell someone your route and turnaround time so search can begin from the right place.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: NPS guidance to stay put and conserve energy when lost.

sourceReferences.title: Lost Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear recommendation to stay put once genuinely lost, with limited safety exceptions.

guides.title: Simple Survival Priorities: What Comes First
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: First 30 Minutes: Stay Oriented and Make a Simple Plan
guides.quickAnswer: For most short-term emergencies, focus on safety, first aid, shelter or warmth, signaling, and then water; food and complex tasks can wait.
guides.whenToUse: When making a quick plan for the next hours after becoming stranded or after an emergency.
guides.preferredAction: Follow a simple order: safety and severe injuries, shelter and temperature, signaling and communication, water, then food and extras.
guides.backupAction: If conditions are mild, adjust the order slightly but still avoid spending early energy on low-value tasks like foraging or elaborate construction.
steps: Check airway, breathing, severe bleeding, and immediate hazards first.
Get out of wind, rain, or direct sun and adjust clothing to avoid hypothermia or heat illness.
Use phone, whistle, mirror, bright clothing, or ground signals early to make yourself findable.
Identify the safest available water source and plan how to treat it.
Conserve energy rather than hunting, foraging, or building complex structures in most short-term events.
warnings: Hypothermia or heat illness can kill in hours, long before hunger.
Treating water is important, but usually less urgent than staying warm or cool.
whatNotToDo: Do not spend your first hour on complex shelters or food gathering if you lack basic shelter or signaling.
Do not ignore obvious hazards while focused on secondary tasks.
Do not assume you must find food immediately.
redFlags: Signs of hypothermia or severe heat illness.
Ongoing severe bleeding, chest pain, difficulty breathing, or altered consciousness.
preparednessTips: Learn basic first aid and CPR through Red Cross or similar training.
Pack an emergency kit that supports shelter, signaling, and water treatment first.
Practice short priority drills on day hikes.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Survival Priorities / Seven Priorities
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Summarizes common survival-priority frameworks around first aid, shelter, signaling, water, and food.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence-based guidance supporting conservative, realistic water priorities in the field.

guides.title: Whistle and Sound Signals (Three-Blast Distress)
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Simple Rescue Signaling
guides.quickAnswer: Use three short whistle blasts repeated regularly as your basic distress signal.
guides.whenToUse: Any time you are lost, injured, or need help and think searchers or other people may be within hearing range.
guides.preferredAction: Blow three short blasts on a whistle, wait 30 to 60 seconds, and repeat.
guides.backupAction: If you have no whistle, shout in patterns, bang metal objects, or use a radio or phone if available.
steps: Carry the whistle on your person so it is always reachable.
Give three strong blasts, each about one second long.
Pause for 30 to 60 seconds to listen and conserve energy, then repeat.
If you hear two blasts or a vocal response, continue giving short blasts every 10 to 15 seconds to guide rescuers in.
If using a radio or phone, keep whistle signaling intermittent and consistent.
warnings: Continuous blowing or yelling can exhaust you and make direction-finding harder for rescuers.
A whistle is less effective in strong wind, near loud rivers, or with helicopter noise.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on your voice.
Do not give random patterns; consistent three-blast signals are easier to recognize as distress.
redFlags: Use whistle signals immediately if anyone is injured, unable to move, or stuck in dangerous terrain.
If no response comes and conditions are worsening, shift more effort toward shelter and self-protection while continuing intermittent signals.
preparednessTips: Carry a pea-less plastic whistle on all trips, attached to your person not just your pack.
Practice the three-blast pattern in safe settings.
Include a whistle in every emergency kit and go-bag.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness and Remote First Aid Pocket Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative Red Cross field guide listing three-whistle-blast emergency signaling.

sourceReferences.title: Lost on the Trail? How a Mirror and Whistle Get You Found
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical explanation of three-blast distress timing and field use.

guides.title: Using a Signal Mirror and Light
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Simple Rescue Signaling
guides.existingGuideReference: Mirror and Light Signaling (Land, Day and Night)
guides.quickAnswer: Aim sunlight or a bright light at aircraft, boats, or distant people using a mirror or reflective object to create intense flashes.
guides.whenToUse: Daytime in open or partly open terrain when you can see aircraft, boats, or distant rescuers, or at night with a flashlight or headlamp.
guides.preferredAction: Use a dedicated signal mirror and aiming method to sweep flashes across potential rescuers.
guides.backupAction: If you lack a signal mirror, use any reflective surface or strobe mode on a light at night.
steps: In sunlight, hold the mirror near your eye and use the V-finger method or built-in sight to walk the reflected spot onto the target.
Sweep flashes slowly across aircraft, ridgelines, trail corridors, or boats and repeat regularly.
At night, use a flashlight or headlamp to send groups of three flashes toward known search directions or moving lights.
If you cannot move much, prop a mirror or reflective blanket where it can catch light passively.
Pair mirror and light signaling with whistle or other methods.
warnings: Mirrors are almost useless without direct or strong indirect light.
Do not shine high-intensity lights into nearby drivers’ or pilots’ eyes at close range.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on a mirror as your only signal.
Do not assume rescuers saw one flash; keep signaling when search activity is nearby.
redFlags: If you see search aircraft, drones, or boats, prioritize signaling over non-essential tasks.
If injured and unable to move, set reflective items where they can work without constant effort.
preparednessTips: Carry a small signal mirror or reflective card and practice aiming it before trips.
Keep your phone screen reasonably clean as a backup reflector.
Choose emergency blankets with a reflective side that can double as a visual signal.

sourceReferences.title: Lost on the Trail? How a Mirror and Whistle Get You Found
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed mirror-signaling methods and realistic field limitations.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness and Remote First Aid Pocket Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative Red Cross field guide listing mirrors and beacons as key signaling tools.

guides.title: Ground, Fire, and Passive Visual Signals
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Simple Rescue Signaling
guides.quickAnswer: Create big, simple, high-contrast shapes on the ground and use bright materials or smoke so rescuers can spot you from air or ground.
guides.whenToUse: When you are staying put or moving slowly and want to attract aircraft, drones, or distant ground searchers.
guides.preferredAction: Use large ground symbols and bright passive markers, and use fire or smoke only when safe and legal.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot make fire or large symbols, hang bright clothing or reflective materials in visible places and keep your area obvious.
steps: Choose an open area visible from above or at a distance if possible.
Use rocks, logs, or gear to make letters or shapes such as SOS, X, or a large arrow at least several feet wide.
If fire is safe and allowed, build small signal fires and be ready to add green material for smoke when searchers are near.
Drape bright clothing or a reflective blanket over rocks or trees to create strong contrast.
At night, group three lights or fires in a line or triangle to signal distress.
Maintain signals and check that they remain visible.
warnings: Fire signaling can be dangerous and may be illegal or reckless in dry or windy conditions.
Smoke can worsen breathing or hide your position if poorly placed.
whatNotToDo: Do not build large fires in high wind, extreme dryness, or near flammable brush.
Do not create cluttered ground markings; simple, large shapes work best.
redFlags: If you see aircraft or hear searchers, focus on boosting visibility quickly.
If fire escapes your control or wind shifts dangerously, move away immediately and reassess.
preparednessTips: Carry a reflective emergency blanket and high-visibility clothing.
Pack at least one reliable fire-starting method if local regulations and wildfire risk allow signal fires.
Learn your area’s fire restrictions before depending on open-flame signaling.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness and Remote First Aid Pocket Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative guide mentioning fires, smoke, mirrors, and whistles for signaling.

sourceReferences.title: How to Signal for Help in the Wild
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical advice on three fires, ground symbols, and layered signaling methods.

guides.title: Choosing the Safest Available Water Source
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Safe Water Collection (Before Purification)
guides.existingGuideReference: Choosing Safe Hydration Sources (Bottled, Tap, Surface Water)
guides.quickAnswer: Pick the clearest, most upstream, and least contaminated-looking source you can, then plan to treat it.
guides.whenToUse: Whenever you must collect natural water for drinking or hygiene in the field.
guides.preferredAction: Select flowing water from higher, cleaner-looking locations and avoid sources near livestock, sewage, or heavy human activity.
guides.backupAction: If only poor-quality sources are available, choose the least bad option and treat it as thoroughly as your tools allow.
steps: Look for flowing water rather than stagnant pools and avoid water with strong odor, scum, or dead animals.
Collect from upstream of camps, trails, or livestock areas whenever possible.
Prefer water that is clear to the eye; if silty, allow sediment to settle before treatment.
Avoid collecting from beaver ponds, irrigation channels, or near latrines and wastewater discharge.
Treat the water before drinking whenever possible.
warnings: Even clear high-mountain water can contain microorganisms.
Contamination risk is higher downstream of agriculture, grazing, or human settlement.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume natural equals safe.
Do not drink from obviously contaminated sources unless you face life-threatening dehydration and have no alternative.
redFlags: Severe vomiting, diarrhea, or dehydration after drinking untreated water.
Known compromise of a public or shared supply after a disaster.
preparednessTips: Carry at least one reliable treatment method on extended trips.
Learn local guidance about water safety in your destination.
Bring enough stored water for the first day where feasible.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence-based medical guidance supporting conservative field water collection and treatment decisions.

guides.title: Collecting and Storing Water Safely Before Treatment
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Safe Water Collection (Before Purification)
guides.quickAnswer: Use the cleanest container you have, avoid dipping with dirty hands or gear, and protect stored water from re-contamination.
guides.whenToUse: Whenever you gather water from natural or emergency sources before you can purify or disinfect it.
guides.preferredAction: Fill dedicated water containers directly from the source or by pouring, minimize contact with dirty surfaces, and separate untreated from treated water.
guides.backupAction: If you lack proper containers, improvise with food-grade bottles, cooking pots, or clean plastic bags and keep them away from waste and fuel.
steps: Rinse containers with source water if they held something else and empty them fully.
Fill containers directly or pour from a cleaner cup or pot rather than dipping with dirty hands.
Keep untreated water separate from treated water.
Cover containers tightly to keep out dirt and insects.
Store in shade if possible and away from fuel, chemicals, or latrine areas.
Treat the water as soon as practical by boiling, filtration, or chemicals.
warnings: Cross-contamination between treated and untreated water is a common failure point.
Some improvised containers may leach chemicals and should not be used.
whatNotToDo: Do not store untreated water in containers that recently held fuel, pesticides, or unknown chemicals.
Do not top off treated water with untreated water without re-treating it.
redFlags: Multiple people develop gastrointestinal illness after drinking from a shared source.
Fuel smell or chemical taste suggesting chemical contamination.
preparednessTips: Include sturdy bottles, a collapsible container, and a treatment method in your emergency kit.
Practice using your filter, tablets, or other methods before an emergency.
For home emergencies, follow public guidance on storing tap water in clean containers.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Technical and evidence-based field water handling and treatment principles.

guides.title: When It’s Better to Risk Untreated Water
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Safe Water Collection (Before Purification)
guides.quickAnswer: If you are at high risk of life-threatening dehydration and have no way to treat water, drinking the best available untreated water may be safer than not drinking at all.
guides.whenToUse: In extreme situations where you cannot treat water and are showing signs of severe dehydration.
guides.preferredAction: Choose the cleanest-appearing source available and drink enough to stabilize, understanding the infection risk.
guides.backupAction: If you can delay briefly, rest, reduce exertion, and see whether you can improvise treatment before deciding.
steps: Assess severe dehydration signs such as dry mouth, minimal urine, dizziness, confusion, or inability to keep walking.
Confirm you truly cannot treat water with fire, fuel, filter, tablets, or safe containers.
Select the least contaminated source available.
Drink slowly at first, especially if nauseated.
After rescue or reaching care, tell medical personnel that you drank untreated water.
warnings: This is a high-risk last-resort choice and can lead to gastrointestinal illness or infection.
Children, older adults, pregnant people, and those with chronic illness may be more vulnerable to waterborne disease.
whatNotToDo: Do not drink clearly hazardous water with visible chemicals, strong fuel odor, or strong sewage smell unless the alternative is likely immediate death from dehydration.
Do not assume a small sip can test safety; pathogens are not detectable by taste.
redFlags: Severe vomiting, diarrhea, blood in stool, fever, abdominal pain, or worsening dehydration after drinking suspect water.
preparednessTips: Always carry a basic water treatment method on outdoor trips.
Store emergency water at home for several days per person.
Learn local emergency guidance on boil-water advisories and chemical spills.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness Medical Society Practice Guidelines for Water Disinfection and Treatment
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Provides the nuanced risk-benefit context behind last-resort untreated water decisions.

guides.title: Hand Hygiene and Food Safety in the Field
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Basic Camp and Bivouac Hygiene
guides.quickAnswer: Clean your hands before eating and after toilet use, and keep food prep surfaces and utensils as clean and dry as possible.
guides.whenToUse: During any multi-hour or overnight stay outdoors, especially when preparing or eating food or after using the toilet.
guides.preferredAction: Wash hands with safe water and soap before eating or handling food and after toilet use; use alcohol-based sanitizer when water is limited.
guides.backupAction: If water is scarce, prioritize sanitizer before eating and after toilet use, and wipe away visible dirt first.
steps: If you have safe water, wash hands with soap for at least 20 seconds and rinse well.
If no water is available, apply alcohol-based hand sanitizer and rub until dry.
Prepare food on the cleanest surface available and keep raw and cooked foods separate.
Wash utensils and dishes with safe water and soap if possible, then air-dry.
Store food away from waste and animals.
warnings: Dirty hands are a major source of gastrointestinal illness in the field.
Some germs are harder to remove with sanitizer alone; soap and water are preferred when available.
whatNotToDo: Do not prepare or eat food immediately after toileting or handling waste without cleaning your hands.
Do not use clearly contaminated water on food-contact items if you can avoid it.
redFlags: Persistent vomiting, diarrhea, or inability to keep fluids down.
Signs of dehydration such as dizziness, confusion, or very low urine output.
preparednessTips: Pack travel-sized soap, alcohol-based sanitizer, and resealable waste bags.
Include lightweight biodegradable soap for dishes and basic washing.
Practice hygiene habits at home so they are automatic in the field.

sourceReferences.title: When and How to Wash Your Hands
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong general handwashing guidance applicable in emergency and field settings.

sourceReferences.title: Camping Hygiene: Tips for Staying Clean Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical limited-water outdoor hygiene guidance.

guides.title: Human Waste and Washing Basics
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Basic Camp and Bivouac Hygiene
guides.quickAnswer: Keep waste and washing well away from water sources and camp, and bury human waste only where local rules allow.
guides.whenToUse: On overnight or multi-day trips without toilets, or when infrastructure fails in disasters.
guides.preferredAction: Use existing toilets or latrines where provided; if not, follow local guidance for catholes and washing at a distance from water.
guides.backupAction: In fragile or heavily regulated areas where burying waste is not allowed, pack out human waste using approved bags per local guidance.
steps: Use toilets or latrines if they exist.
If none exist, choose a site at least about 60 to 70 meters from water, camp, and trails, following local recommendations.
Dig a small cathole 15 to 20 cm deep if allowed and bury waste; pack out toilet paper where required.
Carry wash water away from streams or lakes and scatter greywater broadly instead of dumping it in one spot.
Keep washing and waste areas separate from food storage and sleeping areas.
warnings: Poor waste disposal can contaminate water, attract animals, and spread disease.
Local rules may differ and can override general advice, especially in sensitive or high-use areas.
whatNotToDo: Do not defecate directly in or next to streams, lakes, or springs.
Do not wash dishes or yourself with soap directly in natural water sources.
redFlags: Outbreaks of diarrhea or gastrointestinal illness in your group.
Community or park notices about water contamination.
preparednessTips: Pack a small trowel, toilet paper, hand sanitizer, and waste bags where appropriate.
Learn local regulations before going out; some parks require packing out all waste.
For disasters at home, follow local public health guidance on sanitation and waste disposal.

sourceReferences.title: Camping Hygiene: Tips for Staying Clean Outdoors
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical outdoor hygiene and waste-management advice.

sourceReferences.title: Handwashing in Emergencies
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Emergency hygiene principles that support conservative sanitation practices.

guides.title: Managing Small Wounds and Skin Problems
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Basic Camp and Bivouac Hygiene
guides.existingGuideReference: Caring for Minor Cuts and Scrapes
guides.quickAnswer: Clean small cuts and blisters early, keep them dry and covered, and watch closely for signs of infection.
guides.whenToUse: Any time you get a small cut, scrape, blister, or insect bite in the field.
guides.preferredAction: Rinse with clean or treated water, apply antiseptic if available, and cover with a clean bandage.
guides.backupAction: If you lack antiseptic, rinse with the best available safe water, gently remove dirt, and keep the wound as clean and dry as you can.
steps: Clean your hands or use sanitizer before touching the wound.
Rinse gently with safe water to remove dirt and debris.
Apply antiseptic ointment if available.
Cover with a clean bandage or dressing without cutting off circulation.
Check daily for redness, swelling, pus, warmth, or increasing pain.
For blisters, protect from friction and avoid opening unless necessary for function.
warnings: Even minor wounds can become serious infections in outdoor or disaster settings.
Some ointments and antiseptics can trigger allergic reactions.
whatNotToDo: Do not apply dirt, ash, or unverified natural substances to open wounds.
Do not ignore increasing redness, swelling, or pain.
redFlags: Spreading redness, warmth, significant swelling, fever, or red streaks.
Puncture wounds, animal bites, or deep cuts, especially near joints.
preparednessTips: Carry a basic first aid kit with gloves, dressings, bandages, and antiseptic.
Learn basic first aid from Red Cross or similar organizations.
Keep tetanus immunizations up to date.

sourceReferences.title: When and How to Wash Your Hands
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports the importance of clean hands before wound care.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Supply Kit Checklist
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms basic wound-care items recommended in emergency kits.

guides.title: Tying Down Tarps and Shelters (Taut-Line and Truckers’ Hitch)
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Simple, High-Value Knots and Cordage Uses
guides.quickAnswer: Use adjustable knots that can tension and re-tension guy lines to keep tarps and bivouacs stable.
guides.whenToUse: Any time you need to tension lines for tarps, tents, or improvised shelters.
guides.preferredAction: Use a taut-line hitch on guy lines to create an adjustable loop that slides when slack and grips when loaded.
guides.backupAction: Use simple wraps around trees and multiple half hitches if you do not know the named knots, understanding they are less adjustable.
steps: Wrap cord around a stake or tree and create an adjustable loop in the standing line.
For a taut-line hitch, make two wraps inside the loop toward the anchor and one wrap outside, then tighten.
Slide the knot to adjust tension on the tarp as needed.
For a trucker’s hitch, create a loop in the standing line, run the working end through an anchor point and back through the loop, then pull to tension and secure with half hitches.
warnings: Incorrectly tied knots can slip and cause shelter failure in bad weather.
High loads on thin cord can cut into bark or damage anchors.
whatNotToDo: Do not overload light cordage with heavy loads such as body weight.
Do not rely on camping knots for technical rescue or climbing.
redFlags: Shelter repeatedly collapsing in severe weather when you cannot maintain warmth and dryness.
preparednessTips: Practice a very small set of knots at home until you can tie them under stress.
Carry several meters of strong cord or paracord in your emergency kit.

sourceReferences.title: Knots for Survival and Rescue
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Widely used visual reference for basic, non-technical knots, though it also includes advanced content the app should exclude.

guides.title: Simple Loops and Attachment Knots (Figure-Eight on a Bight, Bowline)
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Simple, High-Value Knots and Cordage Uses
guides.quickAnswer: Use simple, non-slipping loops to attach lines to objects or create fixed loops for gear; do not use them for life-safety loads unless trained.
guides.whenToUse: For attaching gear to packs, hanging food, or creating fixed loops in a line for non-life-critical tasks.
guides.preferredAction: Use a bowline or figure-eight on a bight to create a fixed loop at the end or in the middle of a rope.
guides.backupAction: Use multiple overhand knots and backups if you do not know these knots, recognizing they may be harder to untie.
steps: Tie a bowline by making a small loop, passing the working end up through the loop, around the standing part, and back down through the loop.
Tie a figure-eight on a bight by doubling the rope, forming a loop, wrapping around the standing part to make an 8, then passing the loop through and tightening.
Use these loops for gear attachment or fixed points for non-life-safety tasks.
Dress and tighten the knot carefully before loading it.
warnings: Knots can loosen under repeated loading if not dressed and tightened.
For untrained users, any knot used for life safety is outside safe public guidance.
whatNotToDo: Do not use these knots for rappelling, hauling people, or technical rescue.
Do not assume any knot is safe for life support unless you are trained and using proper systems.
redFlags: If you think you need to move someone on steep terrain with ropes, call professional rescue rather than improvising rope systems.
preparednessTips: Practice these knots under calm conditions.
Include printed or in-app diagrams for quick reference.

sourceReferences.title: Knots for Survival and Rescue
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear visual instruction for simple loops, though the app must filter out advanced rescue and climbing content.

guides.title: Everyday Cordage Uses in Emergencies
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Simple, High-Value Knots and Cordage Uses
guides.quickAnswer: Cordage is most useful for shelter setup, securing gear, simple splints, and improvised repairs—not for climbing or rescue.
guides.whenToUse: In almost any outdoor or disaster scenario where you need to secure, bundle, or support non-life-critical loads.
guides.preferredAction: Use cord to pitch tarps, secure gear, lash broken items, or support simple improvised splints.
guides.backupAction: If you lack dedicated cordage, twist strips of strong fabric or webbing for temporary use.
steps: Identify tasks where cord lowers risk, such as keeping shelter taut, bundling gear, or supporting a simple splint.
Use simple knots like half hitches, taut-line hitches, and bowlines for these tasks.
For splints, pad the injured area, place rigid supports, and tie gently above and below the injury rather than over it.
Check knots and cord tension regularly, especially before bad weather.
warnings: Improvised splints require judgment and poor splinting can worsen injuries.
Thin cord can cause pressure injuries if tied too tightly around limbs.
whatNotToDo: Do not attempt technical rescues, rappels, or body-weight systems with ordinary cordage.
Do not tie anything so tight that skin turns pale, blue, or numb unless you are applying a trained tourniquet for life-threatening bleeding.
redFlags: Signs of reduced circulation below a tied area such as cold, pale, or numb extremities.
Serious fractures, deformity, or inability to move a limb.
preparednessTips: Pack several meters of medium-strength cord or webbing in your kit.
Practice low-risk tasks like hanging tarps or securing bundles.
Include simple illustrated guides for splinting from recognized first-aid organizations.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Supply Kit Checklist
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Illustrates why rope or cord is commonly recommended in emergency kits.

guides.title: Basic Knife Handling and Safe Zones
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs
guides.quickAnswer: Keep your body and others out of your knife’s path, work slowly, and stop using knives when you are cold, exhausted, or impaired.
guides.whenToUse: Any time you use a knife or multi-tool outdoors or in an emergency.
guides.preferredAction: Use controlled cuts away from your body, keep others outside your arm’s-length safety bubble, and sheath the knife immediately when not in use.
guides.backupAction: If you are too cold, tired, or shaky to control the blade, delay the task or use safer alternatives such as tearing, breaking, or using scissors on a multi-tool.
steps: Define your cutting safety bubble at full arm’s reach and keep others out of it.
Avoid cutting in the area between your thighs where a slip can hit major blood vessels.
Cut away from your body and hands using stable support where possible.
Keep blades sharp; dull blades require more force and slip more easily.
Close folding blades and sheath fixed blades immediately after use.
Stop and reassess if you feel rushed, overloaded, or unstable.
warnings: Knife injuries can be life-threatening in remote settings because of bleeding and infection.
Cold, fatigue, and intoxication significantly increase accident risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not use knives while intoxicated or under the influence of drugs.
Do not cut toward your own body or hold material in your lap while cutting.
Do not hand an open knife to another person; close it or pass it handle-first safely.
redFlags: Deep cuts, heavy bleeding that does not stop with direct pressure, or suspected tendon or nerve injury.
Wounds in the groin, inner thigh, or neck.
preparednessTips: Learn basic first aid for bleeding and carry proper dressings.
Choose a sturdy, simple knife or multi-tool with a secure locking mechanism.
Practice safe handling at home before relying on a knife in emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: 7 Crucial Safety Tips When Using Outdoor Knives
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Accessible outdoor knife safety guidance including safe zones and risk factors.

guides.title: Safe Cutting Tasks for Shelter and Repairs
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs
guides.existingGuideReference: Basic Knife Handling and Safe Zones
guides.quickAnswer: Use knives for small, controlled tasks—cutting cord, trimming branches, making stakes—not for heavy chopping or prying.
guides.whenToUse: When you need to create or repair basic shelter elements, prepare simple food, or cut cordage.
guides.preferredAction: Plan cuts, stabilize materials, and work from a safe position, taking breaks to avoid fatigue.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot safely cut, look for alternatives such as breaking branches by hand, using existing debris, or changing the repair approach.
steps: Use the knife to sharpen stakes, cut cord, and trim small branches rather than chop large wood.
For repairs, make controlled cuts to remove frayed material and shape patches instead of using the knife to pry.
For food, cut on a stable surface and keep fingers curled and away from the blade path.
Brace workpieces on the ground or against a solid object instead of holding them loosely.
warnings: Using a knife as a pry bar can break the blade and cause injury.
Heavy chopping with an unsuitable knife adds risk without much benefit in short-term survival settings.
whatNotToDo: Do not attempt to cut large logs or branches when smaller deadfall will work.
Do not carve while walking or distracted.
redFlags: Injury from a broken blade or large splinters.
Lacerations that do not stop bleeding with direct pressure.
preparednessTips: Include a small saw if you anticipate regular shelter-building tasks.
Practice low-risk cutting tasks before emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: 7 Crucial Safety Tips When Using Outdoor Knives
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical, public-facing knife-safety limits and task framing.

guides.title: When Not to Use a Knife
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Knife and Multi-Tool Safety for Shelter, Food, and Repairs
guides.existingGuideReference: Basic Knife Handling and Safe Zones
guides.quickAnswer: Skip the knife if you are cold, exhausted, unstable, or tempted to use it for risky tasks like prying, throwing, or self-defense.
guides.whenToUse: When deciding whether a task truly justifies pulling out your knife.
guides.preferredAction: Ask whether you can solve the problem another way without adding blade risk.
guides.backupAction: If you must use a knife while not at your best, slow down, reduce the task scope, and consider whether a steadier person can do it instead.
steps: Check whether your hands are cold or numb, whether you are very tired, or whether you are emotionally stressed.
Consider alternatives such as moving the shelter, using a different anchor, or breaking branches by hand.
If you proceed, choose the smallest and simplest cut that will work.
Stop after short intervals to reassess your control and need.
warnings: Many serious outdoor knife injuries happen at the end of the day when people are cold and tired.
Panic-driven or tactical uses of knives increase risk without improving safety.
whatNotToDo: Do not use knives for wildlife self-defense plans; follow official wildlife safety guidance instead.
Do not attempt knife skills you have only seen online without prior safe practice.
redFlags: Any deep or heavily bleeding knife wound.
Wounds with loss of movement or feeling in fingers or toes.
preparednessTips: Reduce knife dependence with pre-cut cord lengths or prepared stakes.
Train on safe tool use before extended trips.

sourceReferences.title: 7 Crucial Safety Tips When Using Outdoor Knives
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports conservative, safety-first decisions about whether to use a knife at all.

guides.title: Pocket Always-With-You Kit
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit
guides.quickAnswer: Carry a tiny set of essentials—whistle, light, basic bandages, fire starter, and emergency blanket—on your person, not just in your pack.
guides.whenToUse: Everyday and on any outdoor trip where you might be separated from your main pack.
guides.preferredAction: Assemble a small kit that fits in a pocket or worn pouch and covers signaling, light, basic first aid, and minimal shelter.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot carry a full micro-kit, prioritize a whistle, small flashlight, and a way to contact help.
steps: Include a whistle, small flashlight or headlamp, adhesive bandages, alcohol wipes, gauze, and tape.
Add a compact emergency blanket, lighter or matches if allowed, and a few water treatment tablets where appropriate.
Keep the kit on your body, not only in your backpack.
Check items regularly and replace dead batteries and expired supplies.
warnings: Relying only on a larger kit in your pack can leave you vulnerable if you are separated from it.
Some items such as lighters may be restricted in specific environments.
whatNotToDo: Do not overload the pocket kit so much that you stop carrying it.
Do not assume your phone alone is enough; batteries and signal can fail.
redFlags: If you use critical items such as all your bandages or water tablets, treat that as a cue to shorten the trip or seek safer conditions.
preparednessTips: Adapt official emergency kit guidance to pocket scale.
Customize for local conditions such as extra warmth in cold climates or sun protection in hot climates.

sourceReferences.title: Build an Emergency Kit and Grab-and-Go Bag
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Official checklist that supports small, practical grab-and-go essentials such as light, whistle, and first aid.

guides.title: Daypack-Size Emergency Kit for Hikes
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit
guides.quickAnswer: Add core essentials to your hiking pack: navigation, light, extra clothing, shelter, water, food, first aid, fire, tools, and signaling.
guides.whenToUse: For day hikes, short trips, or local outings where an unplanned night out is possible.
guides.preferredAction: Pack items that let you survive an unplanned night outdoors: extra warmth, rain protection, emergency shelter, water and treatment, first aid, light, and signaling.
guides.backupAction: If space or budget is limited, prioritize shelter or warmth, light, signaling, and water over duplicate tools.
steps: Carry map, compass, and or offline GPS or phone maps.
Pack an extra warm layer, hat, light rain shell, and emergency blanket or bivy.
Bring a headlamp and spare batteries.
Carry enough water for the day plus a treatment method.
Add extra high-energy snacks.
Pack a basic first aid kit, blister care, whistle, reflective item, and a small knife or multi-tool used conservatively.
warnings: Overpacking can lead to leaving the kit behind; find a balance you will actually carry.
Some locations restrict open flame and certain tools.
whatNotToDo: Do not pack only cool gear while neglecting basics like warm clothing or water treatment.
Do not assume group members will share kits; everyone should have basics.
redFlags: If your kit cannot cover an unplanned night in current weather, consider shortening or turning back from the trip.
preparednessTips: Use official emergency kit checklists as a baseline and adapt for hiking.
Review your kit after each trip and adjust based on what you used or missed.

sourceReferences.title: Build an Emergency Kit and Grab-and-Go Bag
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Official checklist that adapts well to an outdoor daypack kit.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong official baseline for water, food, light, tools, and first aid priorities.

guides.title: Grab-and-Go Bag Basics (Home/Urban)
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Packing and Using a Small Emergency Kit
guides.quickAnswer: Keep a small bag ready with water, food, light, communications, first aid, hygiene, documents, and basic tools to leave home quickly.
guides.whenToUse: For evacuations, sudden disasters, or when you must leave home or work on short notice.
guides.preferredAction: Follow official emergency kit guidance and customize for your household.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot assemble a full bag, gather the highest-priority items: medications, water, IDs, a flashlight, and a way to contact family.
steps: Pack non-perishable food and water for several days where feasible.
Add flashlight, batteries or hand-crank light, radio, phone charger, and power bank.
Include first aid, personal medications, and hygiene items.
Add copies of important documents and some cash in small bills.
Include seasonal clothing, an emergency blanket, and a whistle.
Store the bag in an accessible location and review it at least annually.
warnings: A heavy kit may be hard to carry; balance completeness with portability.
Medications expire and must be rotated regularly.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on last-minute packing during an evacuation.
Do not store the kit where it can be blocked in an emergency.
redFlags: If local authorities issue evacuation or shelter-in-place orders, follow them and bring your bag.
If a family member runs out of critical medications, seek medical or pharmacy support quickly.
preparednessTips: Follow local or national emergency preparedness websites for region-specific recommendations.
Include items for children, elders, and pets as needed.
Practice a quick grab-and-go drill with your household.

sourceReferences.title: Build an Emergency Kit and Grab-and-Go Bag
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed official grab-and-go guidance for household evacuation readiness.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong official U.S. emergency kit guidance on water, food, documents, tools, and medicines.

guides.title: Signs You Must Switch to Shelter and Warmth
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guides.quickAnswer: If you are getting cold, wet, or exhausted, stop non-essential tasks and put your energy into staying warm, dry, and out of the wind.
guides.whenToUse: When weather worsens, daylight fades, or you notice early signs of hypothermia or heat stress.
guides.preferredAction: Pause other activities and work on simple, effective shelter and clothing adjustments.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot build shelter, move to more protected terrain, huddle with others, and use any available insulation and emergency blankets.
steps: Watch for uncontrollable shivering, clumsiness, confusion, or slurred speech in cold conditions.
If present, stop tasks like exploring or building elaborate shelters.
Add dry layers, get out of wind or rain, insulate from the ground, and share body warmth if appropriate.
In hot conditions, move to shade, loosen clothing, sip safe water, and rest.
Once basic comfort is restored, re-evaluate priorities and continue only essential tasks.
warnings: Hypothermia and heat stroke can progress quickly and quietly.
Continuing physical work in these conditions can push you into collapse.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep walking just to stay warm if you are wet, exhausted, and unsure of direction.
Do not ignore shivering or confusion in yourself or others.
redFlags: Confusion, loss of coordination, or collapse in cold or hot environments.
Shivering that stops suddenly while the person is still cold.
preparednessTips: Pack adequate clothing and an emergency blanket even on day trips.
Learn signs of hypothermia and heat illness through formal first-aid training.
Check forecasts and plan for worse-than-expected conditions.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports conservative emergency planning and shelter-first behavior when stranded.

sourceReferences.title: How To Avoid, Recognize and Treat Hypothermia
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical exposure-recognition and shelter priorities for public outdoor users.

guides.title: When to Drop Tasks and Focus on Signaling
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guides.quickAnswer: When rescuers are likely searching for you or you see or hear them, stop extra tasks and focus on being seen and heard.
guides.whenToUse: Any time you suspect a search has started or see aircraft, drones, boats, or search teams.
guides.preferredAction: Shift energy to whistle use, mirror or light flashes, and making your location obvious with ground or visual signals.
guides.backupAction: If you cannot actively signal continuously, set up passive signals like bright clothing or reflective blankets where they will be most visible.
steps: When you hear or see potential rescuers, stop non-essential tasks.
Move to a visible area if safe, bringing whistle, mirror, and bright items.
Use three-blast whistle signals and mirror or light flashes toward rescuers.
Stand or place bright gear where you contrast with the surroundings.
Once contact seems likely, stay in place unless directed to move.
warnings: Missing a narrow window of visibility can delay rescue significantly.
Over-focusing on camp tasks can make you miss distant sounds or signs of search teams.
whatNotToDo: Do not continue long, noisy tasks that mask searcher calls or aircraft noise.
Do not hide under dense canopy when you could safely stand in a clearing.
redFlags: If your condition is deteriorating, communicate that clearly as soon as contact is made.
preparednessTips: Keep signaling tools accessible, not buried in your pack.
Establish a group plan so everyone pauses and signals when search activity appears.

sourceReferences.title: Wilderness and Remote First Aid Pocket Guide
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Authoritative field guide stressing signaling early and using whistles, mirrors, and beacons.

sourceReferences.title: Lost on the Trail? How a Mirror and Whistle Get You Found
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical field timing and responsiveness for signaling once a search may be active.

guides.title: Balancing Activity and Rest to Avoid Exhaustion
guides.category: Core Skills and Basic Survival Tasks
guides.parentTopic: Knowing When to Stop Tasks and Focus on Shelter, Warmth, and Signaling
guides.quickAnswer: Pace yourself; overexertion increases risk of injury, poor decisions, and hypothermia or heat illness.
guides.whenToUse: Throughout any extended emergency or survival situation.
guides.preferredAction: Alternate periods of necessary work with rest, and stop tasks before you become exhausted or confused.
guides.backupAction: If you are already exhausted, simplify your plan and focus only on shelter, warmth, and minimal signaling until you recover.
steps: After initial planning, schedule short work blocks followed by rest, especially in difficult conditions.
Keep tasks simple and high-value; avoid complex projects when tired.
Monitor yourself and others for fatigue, irritability, and poor coordination.
Adjust plans as daylight changes and avoid demanding work in the dark unless absolutely necessary.
warnings: Exhaustion and low blood sugar impair judgment.
In cold or wet conditions, activity can hide early hypothermia until you stop moving.
whatNotToDo: Do not insist on finishing non-critical tasks when you are clearly beyond your limits.
Do not work in hazardous terrain when extremely tired or in the dark unless it is the lesser danger.
redFlags: Severe confusion, inability to follow simple instructions, or repeated falls.
Chest pain or shortness of breath not explained by mild exertion.
preparednessTips: Maintain basic fitness and health.
Bring enough food and water to reduce exhaustion and dehydration risk.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports conservative, energy-aware emergency planning once stranded.

sourceReferences.title: Build A Kit
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Reinforces that having supplies reduces the need for risky overexertion in emergencies.
